hook: You already know how to run $t$-tests and ANOVA, but those methods quietly assume your data come from a normal distribution — an assumption that often fails in practice. Nonparametric methods let you draw rigorous conclusions about populations without committing to any particular distributional shape. What can you infer when the normal distribution simply isn't a reasonable model?
concept: The unifying idea is that we can build powerful, valid statistical tests by focusing on ranks, signs, or exact counts rather than on raw numerical values — collectively known as distribution-free inference.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Sign and Rank Tests: Wilcoxon and Mann-Whitney
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to compare medians within paired samples and between two independent groups using rank-based tests that replace the $t$-test when normality cannot be assumed.

Apply the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test to assess whether a sample could plausibly have been drawn from a specified theoretical distribution by measuring the maximum deviation between empirical and theoretical CDFs.

Master Fisher's Exact Test to determine whether two categorical variables are associated in a $2 	imes 2$ contingency table, even when sample sizes are too small for the chi-square approximation to be reliable.
